title: Psalms 40
subtitle: He Put a New Song in My Mouth

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — “He Lifted Me Out of the Slimy Pit”

heb.paragraph: "He put a new song (shîr ḥādāsh) in my mouth, a hymn of praise to our God" (v.3). David waited patiently (v.1), God heard (v.1), lifted him from the pit (v.2), set his feet on rock (v.2), and gave him a new song (v.3). The rescue produces music. Then the stunning declaration: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened" (v.6). Hebrews 10:5–7 applies this to Christ’s incarnation.

refs.note: Hebrews quotes vv. 6–8 directly, applying them to Christ: 'Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but a body you prepared for me.' The psalm's move from ritual sacrifice to obedient will becomes the theology of the incarnation.
The 'slimy pit' and 'mud and mire' imagery (v. 2) recurs in Psalm 69, another Davidic psalm that the New Testament applies to Christ. Both psalms pair rescue language with sacrifice theology.

Hannah's song — 'He raises the poor from the dust' — shares the psalm's theology of divine reversal: God lifts the lowly and sets them on solid ground. Deliverance from the pit is a pattern, not an exception.

notes.note: MacArthur: David's testimony models the pattern of all genuine spiritual experience: patient trust, divine rescue, and resulting praise. The 'new song' is not mere music but a fresh expression of gratitude born from personal experience of God's faithfulness.

MacArthur: the superiority of obedience over sacrifice is a foundational biblical principle (1 Sam 15:22, Hos 6:6, Mic 6:6-8). Hebrews 10:5-10 reveals the ultimate fulfilment: Christ's perfect obedience replaced the entire sacrificial system.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'I waited patiently' — qāwōh qiwwîtî, an intensified construction (infinitive absolute + finite verb). The emphasis is on the quality of waiting: not passive but expectant, not resigned but attentive.

Alter: 'sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened' — the phrase 'dug my ears' (kārîtā) is strikingly physical. God excavated the psalmist's hearing. Obedience required surgical intervention on the capacity to listen.

Calvin: the sequence is crucial: waiting (v.1) → rescue (v.2) → new song (v.3) → many will see and trust (v.3). Calvin: personal deliverance, rightly testified to, becomes evangelistic. One person's story produces communal faith.

Calvin: the priority of obedience over sacrifice (cf. 1 Sam 15:22) is not the abolition of worship but its proper ordering. Calvin: God desires the heart before the offering, the obedient will before the ritual act.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'slimy pit' (bôr šā'ôn) — literally 'pit of roaring' or 'pit of desolation.' The imagery suggests Sheol or a cistern — a deep, dark place from which escape is impossible without external help.

NET: 'my ears you have opened' (literally 'ears you have dug for me') — the meaning is debated. Options: (1) God gave the psalmist the capacity to hear and obey, (2) an allusion to the slave's ear-piercing ceremony (Exod 21:6). The LXX renders 'a body you have prepared for me,' quoted in Heb 10:5.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: three images of rescue — heard my cry, lifted me out, set my feet on a rock. The movement is from attention (hearing) to action (lifting) to establishment (solid ground). Rescue is not complete until the footing is secure.

Kidner: Hebrews 10:5-7 applies these verses to Christ. What the psalmist glimpsed — that God wants obedience, not ritual — Christ fulfilled perfectly. The 'scroll' written about him is the entire redemptive plan.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: 'a new song' (šîr ḥādāš) is a hallmark of eschatological worship in the Psalter (33:3, 96:1, 98:1, 144:9, 149:1). Personal deliverance generates music that anticipates the final redemption song.

VanGemeren: the writer of Hebrews (10:5-10) reads this passage christologically — the obedient one whose body is prepared for sacrifice that supersedes all previous offerings. The psalm's anticipation becomes the gospel's proclamation.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the doubled verb (qāwōh qiwwîtî) emphasises not just patience but the active nature of waiting. Hebrew waiting is not passive sitting but vigilant expectation — like a watchman scanning the horizon for dawn.

Goldingay: 'in the scroll of the book it is written about me' — megillat-sēfer may refer to Torah, to a specific covenant document, or to God's written plan for the psalmist's life. The written word defines the vocation.

hist.context: Psalm 40 combines thanksgiving for past deliverance (vv.1-10) with urgent petition for present distress (vv.11-17), a structure that has led some scholars to see two originally independent psalms joined together (vv.13-17 appear independently as Psalm 70). The 'slimy pit' and 'miry clay' (v.2) draw on ancient Near Eastern imagery of the underworld as a waterlogged cistern — a common metaphor for death and Sheol in Mesopotamian and Ugaritic literature. The declaration 'sacrifice and offering you did not desire' (v.6) represents a prophetic critique of cultic externalism that parallels Isaiah 1:11-17 and Amos 5:21-24.

heb.paragraph: "But as for me, I am poor and needy; may the Lord think of me. You are my help and my deliverer; you are my God, do not delay (ḥūshâ)" (v.17). The psalm’s second half is a lament: troubles surround (v.12), sins overtake (v.12), David asks for swift rescue (v.13). Psalm 70 is nearly identical to vv.13–17 — preserved as an independent psalm.

refs.note: Psalm 70 is virtually identical to vv. 13–17 of this psalm — a standalone extract of the urgent plea section. The duplication confirms this prayer's liturgical importance: it was used independently.

The language 'may all who seek to take my life be put to shame … may those who delight in my vindication shout for joy' closely parallels Psalm 35's concluding imprecation. The two psalms share vocabulary and theological outlook.

notes.note: MacArthur: the transition from praise (vv.1-10) to renewed petition (vv.11-17) illustrates that spiritual maturity does not eliminate the need for ongoing dependence on God. Even after great deliverances, believers face new trials.

MacArthur: the psalm concludes with both corporate worship ('may those who love your salvation always say, "The LORD is great!"') and personal dependence ('I am poor and needy'). Healthy faith combines public praise with private humility.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'my sins have overtaken me' — the second half of the psalm darkens abruptly. After the thanksgiving of vv.1-10, suffering returns. Alter notes this is not contradiction but realism: deliverance does not immunise against future trouble.

Alter: 'I am poor and needy' ('ānî we'ebyôn) — the psalm ends where many laments begin. The psalmist who celebrated rescue now asks for it again. The cycle of need and deliverance is the rhythm of faith.

Calvin: the shift from praise to plea is not inconsistency but honesty. Calvin: the same person who testified to God's past deliverance now faces fresh trouble. Faith remembers past rescue while facing present danger.

Calvin: 'you are my help and my deliverer; do not delay' — Calvin: the urgency is not impatience but dependence. The psalmist has no alternative resource. God is not one option among many; he is the only option.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: these verses appear independently as Psalm 70, suggesting they circulated as a separate lament that was later appended to the thanksgiving psalm. The combination creates a powerful structure: gratitude (1-10) + continued need (11-17).

NET: 'do not delay' ('al te'aḥar) — the Hebrew 'āḥar means to linger, to be slow. The psalmist's final word is a plea for divine speed. Urgency and trust coexist: he trusts God's will but pleads for God's timing.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the return to lament after thanksgiving is theologically important. The Christian life is not a single movement from despair to praise but an ongoing alternation. Each deliverance is real but not final until the last day.

Kidner: 'may those who love your salvation always say, "The LORD is great!"' — the community's ongoing praise is the doxological goal of all individual experience. My story exists to fuel our worship.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the parallel with Psalm 70 shows that Israel reused and adapted prayer texts for different liturgical contexts. The combination with the thanksgiving psalm (vv.1-10) creates a fuller picture of the life of faith: praise and petition intertwined.
VanGemeren: 'I am poor and needy, yet the Lord thinks of me' — 'ānî we'ebyôn is the self-description of the faithful remnant. Poverty before God is not shame but qualification for divine attention.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: 'more than the hairs of my head' — the psalmist's sins are compared to something uncountable. The metaphor is almost comically hyperbolic, yet existentially true: sin feels infinite when guilt is acute.

Goldingay: the psalm ends with tension unresolved — 'do not delay, my God.' This is characteristic of Hebrew prayer: it holds praise and plea, gratitude and urgency, in the same breath. Neither cancels the other.

hist.context: The petition section of Psalm 40 (vv.11-17) describes enemies who seek the psalmist's life and take pleasure in his ruin — standard language of the individual lament that may reflect court intrigue, military threat, or legal persecution. The phrase 'those who say to me, "Aha! Aha!"' (v.15) uses the Hebrew heʾāḥ, an exclamation of malicious delight that appears throughout the Psalter and in Ezekiel's oracles against hostile nations. The repetition of this psalm's petition as Psalm 70 suggests it circulated independently in Israel's liturgical tradition, adapted for different worship contexts.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: One rescued from the pit who proclaims God's faithfulness publicly (vv.1-5,9-10)
ppl.text: David 'waited patiently for the LORD' (v.1) — the Hebrew intensifies by doubling the verb (qavvoh qivviti). God lifted him from a 'slimy pit' and 'miry clay' (v.2), setting his feet on rock — imagery of rescue from chaos and death. The 'new song' God puts in his mouth (v.3) is not just personal gratitude but public testimony designed to inspire others' trust. Verses 6-8, declaring that God desires obedience over sacrifice, are quoted in Hebrews 10:5-7 as Christ's words upon entering the world.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: Rescuer who values heart-obedience above ritual (vv.1-5,11,17)
ppl.text: God's 'wonders' and 'plans for us' are innumerable — 'none can compare with you; were I to speak and tell of your deeds, they would be too many to declare' (v.5). The pivotal revelation: 'Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened' (v.6). The Hebrew 'ears you have dug' (oznayim karita li) suggests God surgically created receptive hearing. This prophetic critique of empty ritual (cf. 1 Sam 15:22, Isa 1:11-17) anticipates the new covenant's internalized obedience.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI proclaim your saving acts in the great assembly; I do not seal my lips, Lord, as you know.ESVI have told the glad news of deliverance in the great congregation; behold, I have not restrained my lips, as you know, O LORD.NIVSacrifice and offering you did not desire— but my ears you have opened— burnt offerings and sin offerings you did not require.ESVIn sacrifice and offering you have not delighted, but you have given me an open ear. Burnt offering and sin offering you have not required.

rows.label: Do Not Delay — 40:11–17
rows.text: "I am poor and needy." Sins overtake. "You are my help — do not delay."

hebtext.note: "He put a new song (shîr ḥādāsh) in my mouth, a hymn of praise to our God" (v.3). David waited patiently (v.1), God heard (v.1), lifted him from the pit (v.2), set his feet on rock (v.2), and gave him a new song (v.3). The rescue produces music. Then the stunning declaration: "Sacrifice and offering you did not desire — but my ears you have opened" (v.6). Hebrews 10:5–7 applies this to Christ’s incarnation.

"But as for me, I am poor and needy; may the Lord think of me. You are my help and my deliverer; you are my God, do not delay (ḥūshâ)" (v.17). The psalm’s second half is a lament: troubles surround (v.12), sins overtake (v.12), David asks for swift rescue (v.13). Psalm 70 is nearly identical to vv.13–17 — preserved as an independent psalm.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: Hebrews 10:5–7 applies "sacrifice you did not desire" to Christ’s incarnation. Worship: the new song from the pit. Mercy: God lifts, sets, and gives music.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'He put a new song in my mouth,' consider asking God whether your worship has grown stale — whether you are still singing old songs of deliverance when He has given you new ones you have not yet learned.
